items.question: Who chooses the loss function in a supervised ML project?
items.answer: The ML engineer or data scientist selects it based on target type (continuous vs discrete), outlier sensitivity, probabilistic goals, and framework conventions — often MSE/MAE for regression and BCE/CE for classification.

items.question: What are MSE, MAE, BCE, and CE?
items.answer: MSE = (y − ŷ)² and MAE = |y − ŷ| score regression error. BCE = −y log ŷ − (1−y) log(1−ŷ) scores binary classification. CE = −log ŷ_y scores multi-class classification with softmax probabilities.

items.question: When do you use MAE instead of MSE?
items.answer: When outliers or mislabeled extremes should not dominate training — MAE penalizes large errors linearly rather than quadratically.

items.question: Where does the loss appear in the training loop?
items.answer: After the forward pass computes ŷ, the loss L(ŷ, y) is averaged over the batch, backpropagated to get gradients on θ, and parameters are updated to reduce that average.

items.question: Why use cross-entropy instead of MSE for classification?
items.answer: BCE and CE match Bernoulli/Categorical maximum likelihood, give strong gradients on wrong classes, and align with probabilistic outputs. MSE on one-hot targets is not the correct likelihood model.

items.question: How does CE relate to H(P, Q)?
items.answer: For one-hot P, H(P, Q) = −log q(y). BCE is the Bernoulli case. The full H(P, Q) definition and Shannon derivation are in the Cross-entropy topic — this topic applies those formulas in supervised training.

items.question: MSE vs MAE for regression — how do penalties differ?
items.answer: MSE squares residuals, so an error of 10 contributes 100 to the sum while an error of 2 contributes 4 — large misses dominate. MAE uses |residual|, so errors 10 and 2 contribute 10 and 2 — linear, more outlier-robust.

items.question: BCE vs CE — when is each used?
items.answer: BCE pairs a single sigmoid ŷ with binary y ∈ {0,1}. CE pairs softmax vector ŷ with one mutually exclusive class label among K options. Multi-label independent tags use K separate BCE heads, not one CE.

items.question: Training loss vs evaluation metric — MSE vs RMSE?
items.answer: MSE is typically minimized during training (smooth, additive over examples). RMSE = √MSE is often reported on test data for interpretability in the same units as y. Same optimum, different scale and role.

items.question: Cause: Model assigns q(y_true) → 0 in CE. Effect?
items.answer: Loss −log q(y) → ∞ and gradients explode, heavily penalizing confident wrong predictions and pushing the model to raise probability on the true class.

items.question: Cause: One extreme outlier in regression training with MSE. Effect?
items.answer: The squared residual dominates the batch average loss and gradients, potentially pulling the model toward fitting the outlier at the expense of typical examples.
